calculated_metrics.confidence_note: This unit cost is estimated by PRISM by dividing total programme expenditure by the total quantified animal beneficiaries.

details.calculation: ($333,448 USD / 160 total beneficiaries) = $2,084.05 USD per outcome. | A $1,000 USD donation achieves ≈ 0.48 outcomes.

calculated_metrics.name: Cost Per Outcome (USD)

calculated_metrics.confidence_note: IES is a Back-of-the-Envelope Calculation (BOTEC) to standardize impact across interventions and species. It relies on philosophical and empirical assumptions.

details.criteria: Pass: <= 2 years of expenditure. | Warn: 2-5 years. | Fail: > 5 years.
Pass: >= 6 months of operating expenses. | Warn: 3-6 months. | Fail: < 3 months.
Pass: At least one intervention is classified as Tier 1 (Systemic Change) or Tier 2 (Prevention & Scalable Care). | Warn: All verifiable interventions are Tier 3 (Direct Care & Indirect Action).

details.calculation: Operates in a low-neglectedness / saturated area (Companion Animals: 100%).
details.criteria: Pass: >= 50% of beneficiaries are high-neglectedness (farmed/wild). | Warn: 1-49% are high-neglectedness. | Fail: 0% are high-neglectedness.

details.calculation: Organisation self-reported on unintended negative impacts.
details.criteria: Bonus: Organisation self-reported on unintended negative impacts. | Not Disclosed: No disclosure found.
details.elaboration: Quote: 'The success of this programme and urbanisation has posed new challenges to the shelter. Strays that were meant to free roam and live out their lives, have been displaced by urbanisation. These older free roaming dogs were captured by authorities or private contractors, due to complaints. Animal welfare groups were asked to take them in and try to rehome them, or their fate will be decided by AVS, which may include euthanasia.'
details.formula: Check for self-reported unintended consequences or failures.

details.calculation: No disclosure of euthanasia or live-release rates found.

details.elaboration: This check rewards transparency for a sensitive operational metric.

source.quote:
Medical Supplies and Veterinary Services
55%
Animal Food and Supplies
18%
Kennel Maintenance & Repairs
12%

This year, 160 lives were changed-each one a dog once forgotten, now seen, loved, and cared for. Whether safe within our shelter, welcomed into a foster home, or supported while roaming free, every dog felt what it meant to matter.

Our objects, as set out in our Memorandum and Articles of Association, are aligned with our mission to:
• Promote compassion for animals through the provision of animal welfare services and prevention of cruelty to animals.
• Rescue, provide food and shelter, and medical care for animals to curtail stray population.
• Promote the adoption of animals, and to render any help regardless of breed.
• Relieve the suffering of animals, whether the suffering is caused by cruelty or otherwise.
• Conduct outreach programs to promote animal appreciation and welfare.

metrics.context_qualifier: in the shelter, via fosterers as well as through stray feeding

source.quote: Mercylight takes care of about 160 dogs, in the shelter, via fosterers as well as through stray feeding.
Mercylight takes care of about 160 dogs, in the shelter, via fosterers as well as through stray feeding.
In 2024, our dedicated team responded to urgent calls for help, rescuing 14 dogs in distress.
In 2024, our dedicated team responded to urgent calls for help, rescuing 14 dogs in distress.
In 2024, 12 dogs found their forever homes-each one a joyful new chapter.
In 2024, 12 dogs found their forever homes-each one a joyful new chapter.

The success of this programme and urbanisation has posed new challenges to the shelter. Strays that were meant to free roam and live out their lives, have been displaced by urbanisation. These older free roaming dogs were captured by authorities or private contractors, due to complaints. Animal welfare groups were asked to take them in and try to rehome them, or their fate will be decided by AVS, which may include euthanasia.
